findings.summary: Phase B ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL construction uses 'enc(sub/secret)' notation without explicit specification that values must be percent-encoded (encodeURIComponent) per the existing /auth/<sub>/<secret> pattern.
findings.evidence: Line 94: '`ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=https://<authHost>/auth/<enc(sub)>/<enc(secret)>`' vs. existing pattern at index.ts:719-731 showing percent-encoding is required before path insertion. Line 249 (Existing patterns) shows the unencoded path matching, but decoding logic (lines 719-731) documents 'encodeURIComponent on the issuer side encodes /,+,= etc.'
findings.suggestion: Explicitly require percent-encoding in Phase B auth source construction: 'ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=https://<authHost>/auth/<encodeURIComponent(sub)>/<encodeURIComponent(secret)>' or equivalent clear directive.

findings.summary: Phase A model registry 'optional env/config override' semantics are ambiguous — unclear whether it replaces the hardcoded registry, augments it, or selects between variants.
findings.evidence: Line 80 & 88: 'codex model registry: hardcoded `gpt-5.6-sol` + alias map (closed grammar, `inline-token.ts` ALIAS pattern), optional env/config override' provides no detail on override behavior. Brainstorm OQ2 (line 109-110) leans on 'hardcode + config override, fail-closed' but plan does not specify semantics.
findings.suggestion: Clarify: 'Config override (env `CODEX_MODELS_OVERRIDE` or file-based) [replaces|augments|selects] the hardcoded registry. If replacement, structure is [...]. Fail-closed: invalid override ignores it and uses hardcoded.'

findings.summary: Phase B /v1/models preflight authentication path is unspecified — plan states 'bearer-gated, both transports' but does not clarify which path cockpit uses to check model existence.
findings.evidence: Line 86: '/v1/models (bearer-gated, both transports): serves the codex registry' and Line 96: 'Preflight: on claudex session create, GET the auth-worker `/v1/models`' does not specify whether cockpit hits canonical /v1/models (header auth?) or path-bearer form (/auth/<sub>/<secret>/v1/models). Existing pattern (index.ts:703-708) shows both forms exist for /v1/messages but plan does not extend this clarity to /v1/models.
findings.suggestion: Specify cockpit preflight auth: 'Cockpit constructs Basic auth header from cloud-bearer.json {sub,secret} and GET /v1/models with Authorization header' OR 'appends /v1/models to ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL for path-bearer form' — match whichever the auth-worker implements.

findings.section: Open questions / OQ1 / Seam / Risk P2
findings.summary: OQ1's promotion argument for the in-worker SSE-transform architecture cites a precedent that doesn't actually test what OQ1 needs tested: the existing claude path is a zero-CPU I/O passthrough, not a per-chunk transform, and no TransformStream-based upstream re-encode exists anywhere in this codebase today.
findings.evidence: packages/auth-worker/src/anthropic-proxy.ts:1094 and :1232 both do `return new Response(upstream.body, {...})` — the claude path forwards the ReadableStream verbatim with zero per-byte JS work. The ONLY existing TransformStream usage in auth-worker is packages/auth-worker/src/index.ts:2533 (`handleQuestionStream`), a self-generated low-frequency (2.5s poll) SSE push, not a relay/transform of an upstream response body. Neither existing pattern exercises the thing OQ1 actually needs proven: sustained per-SSE-event JSON parse + Anthropic-shape rebuild + JSON.stringify CPU cost over a long codex reasoning turn (potentially thousands of small SSE events, some carrying `reasoning.encrypted_content` blobs). The plan's OQ1 lean ("Yes — the existing claude path streams long turns through this same worker daily") and Risk P2 ("far under the paid-plan CPU budget") assert this without a back-of-envelope calc, and no `cpu_ms` override exists in packages/auth-worker/wrangler*.toml (checked: none present), so the worker runs on the CF platform default. Verification is deferred entirely to Phase C live smoke — AFTER Phase A's full translator module, golden fixtures, and tests are already built — even though the plan's own cost-if-wrong is "high (architecture redo to out-of-worker)".
findings.suggestion: Add a synthetic CPU-time/wall-time load test to Phase A's own test bullet: feed translate-response.ts's TransformStream a large synthetic OpenAI Responses SSE fixture (thousands of small events, including a large encrypted-reasoning-content event) and measure elapsed CPU/wall time locally (vitest + `performance.now()`, or a `wrangler dev` timing harness) against the worker's actual configured CPU budget, before Phase A is declared complete — not only at Phase C live smoke.

findings.summary: The credential-carve-out plan ("mirror its upsert/idMint shape for `openai` rather than forcing it through PROVIDERS") cites a precedent that doesn't exist for the HTTP connect endpoint it names — the actual `'codex'` account precedent uses a completely different code path (local device-OAuth + /v1/sync), not the api-key connect handler.
findings.evidence: The only existing api-key connect endpoint, `handleApiKeyRegister` (packages/auth-worker/src/index.ts:1905-1990, routed at index.ts:1142-1143 `POST /v1/accounts/api-key`), hard-gates at index.ts:1933 (`if (typeof provider !== 'string' || !Object.hasOwn(PROVIDERS, provider))`) BEFORE any idMint/upsert logic runs — there is no branch in this handler that bypasses the PROVIDERS check for a non-registry provider to mirror. `provider:'codex'` accounts (types.ts:58, `ProviderId | 'codex'`) are never added through this endpoint at all; they arrive via a separate local `packages/auth-service` device-code OAuth flow synced up through `/v1/sync` (per codex-token.ts:1-25's own doc comment and auth-worker-do.ts:632). The underlying DO ingest `/v1/internal/add-account` (auth-worker-do.ts:1798-1814) IS provider-agnostic (`this.accounts.upsertAccount(account as AuthAccount)`, no registry check) — so a fix is buildable — but the plan's own description points an implementer at a precedent ("mirror its upsert/idMint shape") that does not exist for this HTTP surface, and the `idMint` helper it would need (`mintApiKeyId`, providers.ts:491) is unexported and typed to `ProviderId` only, excluding `'openai'`.
findings.suggestion: Spell out the actual mechanism explicitly in Phase A: add a `provider === 'openai'` special-cased branch inside `handleApiKeyRegister` (index.ts:1929-1945) that skips the `Object.hasOwn(PROVIDERS, provider)`/`authStrategy` checks for that one value, manually constructs the account id (mirroring `mintApiKeyId`'s `<provider>-<accountId>` shape as a standalone inline helper, since the registry's version is inaccessible), and calls the same `/v1/internal/add-account` ingest the rest of the function already uses.

findings.summary: The fail-closed contract ("codex-shaped but unregistered model → structured 400, never routed to Anthropic") requires a model-name SHAPE heuristic distinct from exact-registry-match, but neither Phase A's `models.ts` description nor any existing codebase pattern defines one — without it this named Success Signal cannot be implemented as specified.
findings.evidence: Phase A bullet describes `isCodexModel(model)` only in terms of "hardcoded gpt-5.6-sol + alias map (closed grammar, inline-token.ts ALIAS pattern)" — an EXACT-match registry lookup. `inline-token.ts`'s ALIAS pattern (verified: inline-token.ts:45-50, `Object.hasOwn(ALIAS, inner)`) is exact-match only; there is no shape/prefix heuristic anywhere in this codebase to mirror. If `isCodexModel()` only does exact-match, then a codex-shaped-but-unregistered model (e.g. an operator typo, or a newer codex model id not yet added to the closed registry) returns `false` and — per the plan's own stated detection seam ("isCodexModel(body.model) → codex branch; else existing path untouched") — falls through to the EXISTING Anthropic path, directly contradicting Success signal #4 ("never routed to Anthropic, never silently substituted; asserted by test") and Decision 4 ("explicitly do NOT reuse resolveTargetProvider's unknown→claude default").
findings.suggestion: Define the shape heuristic explicitly in Phase A's models.ts spec (e.g. a regex over known OpenAI/codex model-id prefixes: `gpt-`, `o1-`/`o3-`/`o4-`, `codex-`) as a SEPARATE `looksLikeCodexModel(model)` predicate from the exact-registry `isCodexModel(model)`, so the detection seam can distinguish (a) registered codex model → codex branch, (b) codex-shaped-but-unregistered → structured 400, (c) neither → existing Anthropic path untouched.

findings.summary: The claimed detection seam location ("the DO's /v1/messages handling where the body text is already read... one conditional, one call site") is inaccurate — the DO handler passes the raw, unconsumed Request straight into proxyToAnthropic, which reads the body itself; implementing model-keyed detection requires an extra request.clone()+parse step the plan doesn't mention.
findings.evidence: auth-worker-do.ts:806-808 calls `proxyToAnthropic({ accounts: this.accounts, request, ... })` passing the raw Request object — the DO's own `/v1/messages` block (auth-worker-do.ts:735-866) never reads or parses the body itself. The body is instead read inside `proxyToAnthropic` via `await request.text()` at anthropic-proxy.ts:498. For the DO to decide codex-branch-vs-existing-path BEFORE calling either `proxyToAnthropic` or the new codex-upstream module, it must `request.clone()`, read+JSON-parse the CLONE to peek `body.model`, then dispatch the ORIGINAL untouched Request to whichever branch runs — since a Request body can only be consumed once. This is buildable (Workers Request.clone() is standard) but is a distinct, unstated mechanical step, not the "body text is already read" the plan describes.
findings.suggestion: Update Phase A's detection-seam bullet to specify the `request.clone()` peek explicitly, and add a test asserting the byte-identical claude-path regression still holds when the clone-then-dispatch flow is in place (guards against a careless implementation that reconstructs a new Request from re-stringified JSON instead of forwarding the truly original object).

findings.summary: The stated tmux minimum version ("tmux ≥3.0 for -e on new-window/new-session") is factually wrong for the new-session case — `-e` was added to new-window/split-window in tmux 3.1 and to new-session specifically in tmux 3.2, and createWindow's cold-start bootstrap path (no server running) uses new-session.
findings.evidence: Verified against the tmux 3.6a CHANGES file shipped with this host's Homebrew install (/opt/homebrew/Cellar/tmux/3.6a/CHANGES): the "CHANGES FROM 3.1c TO 3.2" section contains "Add -e flag for new-session to set environment variables, like the same flag for new-window" (implying new-window already had it); the "CHANGES FROM 3.0a TO 3.1" section contains "Add a -e flag to new-window, split-window, respawn-window, respawn-pane to pass environment variables". Neither entry appears in the 3.0/3.0a sections. claude-cockpit's lib/tmux.js:388 (`createWindow`'s no-server bootstrap) and lib/tmux.js:581 (`createTmuxSession`) both call `new-session -d`, which needs tmux ≥3.2 for `-e` support — not ≥3.0 as the plan states.
findings.suggestion: Correct the Invisible-dependencies line to "tmux ≥3.2" and add it to the cloud-mode-deps or a Phase B pre-flight check (e.g. `tmux -V` version gate with a clear error) so an operator on an older tmux gets a diagnosable failure rather than a cryptic "unknown flag -e" from execFile on first cold-start session creation.

findings.section: Phase B — cockpit Claudex agent / Significant decision #7 (picker default flip)
findings.summary: Phase B's file scope (lib/models.js, server.js, lib/tmux.js, lib/config.js) omits the React/TS `web/` SPA, which is the ONLY functioning new-session UI in claude-cockpit — so the plan's own 'Picker/default flip' bullet cannot be delivered by the files it names.
findings.evidence: Read `~/Projects/claude-cockpit/public/app.js` in full (826 lines): it has zero session-creation code (no `/api/session/new` call, no agent/model picker) — it only lists existing sessions and replies. Session creation lives in `web/src/components/NewSessionDraft.tsx` (`const [agent, setAgent] = useState<'claude' | 'codex'>(...)`, imports `createSession`/`getModels`/`fetchSpawnAgents` from `web/src/lib/api.ts`), `web/src/components/NewSessionForm.tsx` (`defaultAgentForFilter(): 'claude' | 'codex'`, `FILTER_TITLE` closed Record), and `web/src/components/SessionRail.tsx` (`export type SessionFilter = 'all' | 'agents' | 'claude' | 'codex' | 'terminal'`). `web/package.json` confirms this is a separate React 19 + Vite + TypeScript app with its own build (`web/src/lib/models.js`'s own JSDoc even says 'the SPA fetches it via /api/models rather than hardcoding a copy'). None of these files, or the closed-union-type pattern they use, appear in Phase B's bullets, `adopted-patterns:`, or the Significant Decisions #7 citation list (which cites only `server.js:1210,1226-1230,1161-1164`).
findings.suggestion: Add explicit web/src scope to Phase B: extend the `'claude' | 'codex'` unions in NewSessionDraft.tsx/NewSessionForm.tsx/SessionRail.tsx (and the API client types in lib/api.ts) to a third `'claudex'` value, wire the model dropdown to the new claudex model list, and implement the 'Legacy' grouping in the picker UI. Cite these files (or the closed-union pattern in NewSessionForm.tsx) in `adopted-patterns:` alongside the Node-side seams already listed, and fold the extra TSX/TS files into the file/LOC budget.

findings.section: Phase A — auth-worker codex upstream / credential class (openai connect carve-out)
findings.summary: The 'openai carve-out on the api-key connect path' (Phase A bullet 5) describes real new registration logic but names no file or function for it, even though the existing handler it must bypass (`handleApiKeyRegister`, index.ts:1905-2010) hard-gates on `Object.hasOwn(PROVIDERS, provider)` — a check 'openai' will never pass by design (Decision #1).
findings.evidence: packages/auth-worker/src/index.ts:1933-1945 rejects any provider not in `PROVIDERS` with `UNKNOWN_PROVIDER`, then calls `PROVIDERS[provider].idMint(...)` (line 1939, 1962) — both steps are inapplicable to `openai` per the plan's own Decision #1 (no ProviderDescriptor for codex/openai). The plan's Phase A file list (models.ts, translate-request.ts, translate-response.ts, proxy.ts) has no slot for the mirrored registration path, and the bullet text ('mirror its upsert/idMint shape') implies duplicating id-minting + upsert + 409-idempotency logic (~50-80 lines) without naming where it lives.
findings.suggestion: Name the carve-out explicitly (e.g. a small `openai-connect.ts` module or a documented fork inside `handleApiKeyRegister`) in Phase A's file list, and state whether it duplicates or factors out the shared upsert/label/expiry logic from `handleApiKeyRegister` so the implementer doesn't silently copy-paste ~80 lines.

reviews.overall_note: Scope is mostly well-guarded (S1-S3 already forbid a generic multi-provider framework, wholesale CLIProxyAPI port, and generic cockpit config — good self-discipline) and feature tier holds; the one real gap is that Phase B's scope enumeration only covers claude-cockpit's Node-side files and misses the React/TS `web/` SPA that actually implements the new-session picker Decision #7 depends on.

findings.summary: Decision #6's ref `claude-cockpit/lib/tmux.js:123-126` is a real, verbatim-matching citation but supports an unrelated claim — it's the `TMUX_ENV`/`LC_ALL` locale-normalization constant passed to Node's `execFile` (fixes tmux's `-F` field-delimiter parsing under non-UTF-8 locales), not evidence about tmux's pane-level `-e` env-injection flag being absent from `new-window`/`new-session` calls.
findings.evidence: Plan row: '`claude-cockpit/server.js:1364-1392; claude-cockpit/lib/tmux.js:123-126 has no injection today` [ref]'. Actual file content at those lines: `const TMUX_ENV = { ...process.env, LC_ALL: process.env.LC_ALL || process.env.LANG || 'en_US.UTF-8' };` (child-process env for the tmux binary itself). The functions that actually construct `new-session`/`new-window` args without any `-e` flag are `createWindow` (tmux.js:358-419) and `createTmuxSession`/`createWindowInSession` (tmux.js:559-630) — a grep for the literal `-e` flag string in tmux.js only turns up an unrelated `send-keys -e` escapes flag at line 930.
findings.suggestion: Repoint the decision-table ref to the actual `createWindow`/`createTmuxSession` arg-construction sites (tmux.js:358-419, 559-630) that lack an `-e` option today — or drop the tmux.js line citation and keep only the server.js:1364-1392 ref. Non-blocking: Phase B's own bullet already correctly names `createWindow`/`createTmuxSession` as the functions to grow an env option, so implementer behavior is unaffected — this is purely a supporting-evidence mismatch in the decisions table.

reviews.overall_note: All 6 entries in ## Existing patterns referenced are real (verified byte-for-byte against the worktree/cockpit repo at the cited paths, well within line-range tolerance) and pass the mechanical relevance test (M2/M3) — no hallucinated paths, no snippet drift, no pattern-matched-but-unrelated files; feature tier's >=3 threshold is cleared 2x over (6 valid entries). Spot-checked 9/9 checkable [ref:] citations in the Significant decisions table (skipped 2 external-repo refs, not codebase-checkable): 8 are precise and directly support their claims (providers.ts:1-23, index.ts:703-765, index.ts:1915-1985+types.ts:58, anthropic-proxy.ts:389-399, server.js:1364-1392, server.js:1210/1226-1230/1161-1164, selector.ts:52-87, and the grep-verified 'no /v1/models route today' claim); one (tmux.js:123-126) is a real-but-irrelevant citation, flagged above at P2/non-blocking since the plan's actionable Phase B guidance elsewhere correctly names the real target functions.

findings.summary: The claudex-injected bearer secret (embedded as raw URL path segments in ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL) may be echoed back into the visible, remotely-viewable tmux pane by Claude Code itself, and cockpit has no redaction on that capture path.
findings.evidence: Plan decision #6/T3: 'ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=https://<authHost>/auth/<enc(sub)>/<enc(secret)>' set via tmux -e at window creation; mitigation claims 'cockpit redacts the URL in any error output' but — unlike every other decision row — cites no file:line seam for that redaction, and none exists: /Users/ernie/Projects/claude-cockpit/lib/tmux.js capturePane() (lines 923-943) returns tmux `stdout` verbatim with no transform, and this is exactly the function LivePane/transcript surfaces read from. /Users/ernie/Projects/claude-cockpit/lib/auth.js confirms cockpit is designed for non-local access (durable persisted token, 'phone URL' framing) and runs fully open when no token is configured. The plan's own precedent (packages/auth-worker/src/index.ts:2076, handleAnthropicTokenIssue's `anthropic_base_url_hint`) shows this exact secret-in-URL shape is only previously used by headless/programmatic consumers (RemoteCredentialBackend, sdk-env-merge.ts for the Agent SDK) — never by an interactive TTY CLI that a human watches, which is what Phase B newly introduces for cockpit.
findings.suggestion: Before Phase B ships, verify the target Claude Code build (>=2.1.193) never prints ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL (or the request URL) to stdout/stderr under any reachable interactive code path (startup banner, /status, `claude doctor`, connection-failure/error messages). If it can, add secret-redaction to cockpit's pane-capture pipeline (capturePane / LivePane / transcript tailer) for the `/auth/<sub>/<secret>/` path shape, since that is the actual surface any cockpit viewer (including remote/phone-URL clients) would see it through — not just cockpit's own synthetic error responses.

findings.summary: The plan doesn't pin which auth gate the new 'openai' provider carve-out on the api-key connect path preserves, in an area with three distinct, non-interchangeable auth modes (SSO / internal-secret / path-bearer).
findings.evidence: Plan Phase A: 'openai carve-out on the api-key connect path (the existing handler rejects non-registry providers — mirror its upsert/idMint shape for openai rather than forcing it through PROVIDERS)'. handleApiKeyRegister (packages/auth-worker/src/index.ts:1905-2022) gates on `identity.mode !== 'sso'` (line ~1911, 401 for non-SSO) BEFORE the `Object.hasOwn(PROVIDERS, provider)` check the plan explicitly says to bypass for openai — the plan names the check to skip but is silent on which checks (SSO gate, apiKey non-empty, label cap) the new sibling path keeps.
findings.suggestion: Pin explicitly in Phase A that the openai carve-out preserves the same `identity.mode === 'sso'` gate (plus apiKey/label validation) as the existing PROVIDERS-based path, so the endpoint that mints a billed-credential AuthAccount can't accidentally be reachable via the internal-secret or path-bearer auth modes used elsewhere in this same file.

findings.summary: The Phase A /v1/models registry is explicitly static (hardcoded model list, pure predicate) and therefore can't reflect whether an operator actually has a usable `provider:'openai'` vault credential — so cockpit's preflight ('model absent -> 400') can pass while the first real /v1/messages turn still fails on missing/exhausted credentials.
findings.evidence: Plan Phase A: 'codex model registry: hardcoded gpt-5.6-sol + alias map ... exports isCodexModel(model) (pure predicate) + registry listing for /v1/models' — registry-only, not account-aware. The existing parallel pattern (handleCodexTokenIssue, packages/auth-worker/src/auth-worker-do.ts:2451-2457) shows credential-absence is only discovered at issue-time (404 no_codex_credentials / 429 all_codex_rate_limited), never surfaced at a model-list step. Phase B's stated preflight only checks 'requested model absent -> 400 with the served list', which the fail-closed model gate in decision #4/T2 does not extend to.
findings.suggestion: Either make /v1/models reflect live per-operator credential availability for the codex entries, or have Phase C's fail-closed verification explicitly exercise the 'registered model, no/exhausted openai credential' case and confirm /v1/messages returns a clear structured error (not a generic 500/502) distinguishable from the unregistered-model 400 — so the fail-closed contract covers credential-absence, not just model-registry-absence.

findings.summary: T6's OpenAI error handling is purely reactive (429/401/5xx cooldown, mirroring the claude account-cooldown state machine) — no proactive spend/request cap is named anywhere for the billed OpenAI credential, unlike Claude's fixed-cost subscription accounts this custody pattern was borrowed from.
findings.evidence: Decision #3 justifies vault-account custody as giving '429 accounting + credential rotation parity with claude'; T6: 'small OpenAI-flavored classifier writing the SAME account cooldown fields'. This machinery only engages after OpenAI itself starts throttling — it bounds request-rate the same way the claude subscription path does, but a claude subscription account has a fixed monthly cost regardless of usage while a billed OpenAI API key accrues real, uncapped-by-this-plan cost per token. Nothing in Phase A/C names a proactive ceiling (request count, token budget, or pointer to OpenAI's own dashboard spend limits as the documented control).
findings.suggestion: Add one explicit line to Phase A or the Phase C runbook naming the spend-safety story for the billed key — e.g., document that the operator sets OpenAI dashboard hard/soft spend limits as the primary control, and/or add a request or token ceiling per cooldown window (reusing the existing `usage.requestCount5h` counter noted as in-scope-lite) so a translator bug or a hung tool-loop can't generate unbounded billing before a human notices.

findings.summary: Row 6's Considerations cell cites lib/tmux.js:123-126 as evidence 'no injection exists today,' but that range is TMUX_ENV (LC_ALL locale-forcing for the tmux CLI child process itself), unrelated to pane/session env injection — the actual 'no injection' evidence is that createWindow/createTmuxSession take no env param.
findings.evidence: claude-cockpit/lib/tmux.js:123-127 (`const TMUX_ENV = {...process.env, LC_ALL: ...}`, consumed by runTmux's own execFile call) vs. createWindow signature at :358 (`{ cwd, name } = {}, { _run, _listPanes } = {}`) and createTmuxSession at :559 — neither accepts an env/`-e` option today, which is the citation that actually supports the row's claim.
findings.suggestion: Repoint the citation to the createWindow/createTmuxSession signatures (~lib/tmux.js:358, :559) rather than the LC_ALL constant at :123-126.

findings.section: ## Significant decisions — row 7 (Claudex = new `claudex` agent kind)
findings.summary: Considerations cell names zero alternatives — it restates the existing agent-union/model-allowlist mechanism and cites the LOCKED decision, but never weighs the plausible alternative of extending `agent==='claude'` with a model-triggered/flag-based base-URL injection (cockpit already has this exact sub-flag pattern for codexTransport).
findings.evidence: server.js:1210 (`agent ∈ {'claude','codex'}`), :1215-1218 (`codexTransport ∈ {'tmux','rpc'}` — an existing same-agent sub-selector pattern that a claudex flag could have mirrored instead of a 3rd top-level agent value); lib/config.js defaults() shows no ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL is set for today's claude launches at all, which is the real reason a distinct kind is warranted — but that reasoning isn't in the cell.
findings.suggestion: Add one clause naming and rejecting the flag-based alternative, e.g.: 'not a claude-agent sub-flag, because existing claude sessions must default to the operator's own subscription auth, never silently to the vault/billed-OpenAI path — the split has to be an explicit top-level choice.'

findings.summary: Considerations cell justifies the new server-side endpoint only as 'needed for preflight' but never engages the precedent it diverges from: CLAUDE_MODELS/CODEX_MODELS are pure client-hardcoded catalogs today with zero server round-trip, and the cell doesn't say why codex needs one when neither existing agent does.
findings.evidence: claude-cockpit/lib/models.js:40 (CLAUDE_MODELS), :59 (CODEX_MODELS) — both hardcoded, no existing preflight/introspection call anywhere in the codebase (grep-confirmed no `/v1/models`-shaped route today, matching the row's own citation).
findings.suggestion: Name the rejected alternative explicitly: 'client-hardcoded-only (matching CLAUDE_MODELS/CODEX_MODELS) was rejected because the server registry is config-overridable (Phase A), so a static cockpit list could silently drift from what's actually served — defeating the fail-closed guarantee.'

findings.summary: T6 ([known]) commits to a real, non-obvious design choice — the OpenAI 429/401/5xx classifier writes the SAME account cooldown fields the Anthropic classifier uses, rather than adding parallel OpenAI-specific bookkeeping — but this choice never appears as a row in the decisions table.
findings.evidence: ## Risk candidates T6: 'small OpenAI-flavored classifier writing the SAME account cooldown fields (reuse the state machine semantics at anthropic-proxy.ts:931-1068 as the reference shape, not the code)'.
findings.suggestion: Add a row: 'OpenAI 429/401/5xx classifier reuses the Anthropic path's account cooldown/state-machine fields verbatim rather than a parallel OpenAI-specific state model' — considerations: a second cooldown model would fork the picker/dashboard logic that already reads those fields; importance: keeps rotation/dashboard UX identical across providers.

findings.summary: The plan already commits, in Phase A prose and the P1/P2 risk mitigations, to an incremental (streaming) TransformStream SSE re-encode that never buffers a full response — and OQ1 calls this choice load-bearing enough to be 'Halt-B territory' if wrong. That's a Significant-decision-grade architectural bet, not just an assumption, yet it has no row.
findings.evidence: Phase A: 'Responses SSE → Anthropic SSE as an incremental TransformStream state machine'; Risk P1: 'incremental TransformStream (no full-response buffering) is the design constraint that makes this achievable'; OQ1: 'Sits on the Seam; if wrong, the in-worker translator premise fails → Halt-B territory'.
findings.suggestion: Add a row: 'SSE re-encode is an incremental streaming TransformStream, never buffering a full response, to stay inside Workers CPU/memory limits on long codex turns' — considerations should name the rejected alternative (buffer full response then translate — simpler code, but risks Workers memory/CPU limits on long turns, which is exactly what OQ1/P1/P2 worry about).

findings.summary: Brainstorm OQ2 explicitly posed 'hardcode gpt-5.6-sol vs. dynamically fetch OpenAI's models list' as an open trade-off; the plan silently resolves it to 'hardcoded + config-overridable' in Phase A prose without a decision row, and it's also absent from the plan's own carried-forward Open Questions (OQ1-OQ7).
findings.evidence: brainstorm ~/.claude/brainstorms/claudex-integration.md OQ2: 'model registry — hardcode gpt-5.6-sol (+ alias map) v1, or fetch OpenAI models? Lean: hardcode + config override, fail-closed.' vs. plan Phase A bullet 1: 'codex model registry: hardcoded gpt-5.6-sol + alias map... optional env/config override' — resolved with no row and no OQ carryover.
findings.suggestion: Add a row: 'Codex model registry is a hardcoded closed grammar (gpt-5.6-sol + alias map, config-overridable) rather than dynamically fetched from OpenAI's /v1/models' — considerations should name the rejected dynamic-fetch alternative and why (extra runtime dependency on OpenAI's models endpoint for a v1 with exactly one supported model id).

reviews.overall_note: Table is well-grounded overall (7 of 9 rows re-verify cleanly against the actual code, including several citations I independently checked) but has 3 rows with real gaps — one mis-cited evidence line (row 6), and two rows (7, 9) that describe the choice made without naming or rejecting the plausible alternative — plus 3 architecturally significant [known]-risk / brainstorm-OQ resolutions that never made it into the table at all.
